ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for claypot chicken; the closest parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03301521) is used as the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific claypot chicken entry; 'Chicken meat, cooked' (A0CKM) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for this braised chicken dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for claypot chicken as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of claypot chicken is approximately 200g, representing one individual portion as served at a hawker centre or home meal, including chicken pieces with skin and bone and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Claypot Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Claypot Chicken (cr_id F11020025), confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Claypot chicken has no direct GI measurement in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous profile of braised chicken with soy sauce: very low carbohydrate content (1.03g/100g), high protein (11.75g/100g), and moderate fat (6.54g/100g). Protein and fat substantially blunt glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (35 × 1.03 × 2) / 100 = 0.72, rounded to 0.7. This is consistent with published GI values for low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 568mg per 100g — primarily from dark soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid claypot chicken as prepared, due to sodium at 568mg/100g; request reduced soy sauce, substitute low-sodium soy sauce, and limit portion to 100g to keep single-meal sodium contribution below 600mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.03g/100g) and high protein (11.75g/100g) minimise glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Claypot chicken is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content (1.03g/100g) and high protein (11.75g/100g), which together produce a minimal glycaemic response; pair with low-GI staples such as brown rice and monitor total sauce volume to limit added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (568mg/100g) and moderate fat (6.54g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~1.8g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, claypot chicken should be consumed in small portions (≤100g) with reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, as the high sodium content (568mg/100g) poses a blood-pressure risk; the moderate fat level and low saturated fat (~1.8g/100g) are acceptable, but total dietary sodium from all sources must be monitored.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 568mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; anti-inflammatory ingredients (ginger, garlic, dried mushrooms) are beneficial
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Claypot chicken contains anti-inflammatory ingredients (ginger, garlic, dried mushrooms with beta-glucans) that are beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis, but sodium at 568mg/100g exceeds the recommended threshold; request reduced soy sauce and avoid if on methotrexate due to trace alcohol from Shaoxing wine.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Claypot Chicken (砂锅鸡, Sha Guo Ji) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean braised chicken dish cooked in a traditional clay pot. Bone-in, skin-on chicken pieces are marinated and slow-braised with dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, oyster sauce, ginger, garlic, green onions (spring onions), sesame oil, and Shaoxing rice wine, often with dried shiitake mushrooms. The clay pot retains heat evenly and imparts a subtle earthy aroma. The resulting dish has tender, deeply flavoured chicken with a rich, savoury-sweet dark sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 110 kcal, 11.75g protein, 6.54g fat, and only 1.03g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. Sodium is elevated at 568mg/100g due to soy and oyster sauces. It is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its minimal glycaemic impact, but requires sodium reduction for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Commonly served at hawker centres and Chinese restaurants in Singapore as a dinner dish, often accompanied by steamed white rice or porridge.
common_user_queries: How many calories in claypot chicken?
Is claypot chicken suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat claypot chicken if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of claypot chicken?
Is claypot chicken high in protein?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11020025) with confidence 0.95, representing an exact match to the canonical Singapore food record for claypot chicken. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for claypot chicken is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for claypot chicken as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero trans fat for this braised chicken preparation. potassium_mg estimated at 220mg/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for braised chicken with skin (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB does not report potassium for this entry. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.80g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central braised chicken with skin analogue; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate (low category) based on the very low carbohydrate content (1.03g/100g) of this dish; no direct GI measurement exists for claypot chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dietary tags in the input included 'plant_based' and 'keto' which are not accurate for this dish (contains chicken); these have been corrected. Halal tag retained as claypot chicken is commonly prepared halal in Singapore hawker settings, though Shaoxing wine is a traditional ingredient — halal versions substitute with non-alcoholic alternatives.
